words.shortDefinition: dealing sensibly with practical realities
words.simpleDefinition: Choosing what works in the real world over what's theoretically perfect.
words.longDefinition: Pragmatic decisions weigh constraints — time, budget, team capacity — alongside ideals. Pragmatism rejects purity for its own sake when ship-quality is good enough.
words.professionalContext: A pragmatic engineer ships the imperfect solution today rather than the perfect one in six months.
words.exampleSentence: Her pragmatic approach favored a feature flag over a full architectural rewrite.
words.etymology: From Greek pragma, 'thing done, deed' — pragmatism focuses on what's actually done, not what's theorized.
words.connector: Like duct tape that holds the launch together — not pretty, but it works.

words.shortDefinition: able to be acted on; providing clear next steps
words.simpleDefinition: Specific enough that someone knows exactly what to do.
words.longDefinition: Actionable feedback or data tells the recipient what step to take next. 'The site is slow' is observation; 'cache the homepage API response' is actionable.
words.professionalContext: Code review comments should be actionable — not 'this could be cleaner' but 'extract this into a helper'.
words.exampleSentence: Her actionable retro notes turned into three tickets the same day.
words.etymology: From Latin actio, 'a doing' + the suffix -able. Anything you can act on.
words.connector: Like a map with the route highlighted — not just the territory.

words.shortDefinition: involving repetition; refining through cycles
words.simpleDefinition: Improving something step by step instead of all at once.
words.longDefinition: Iterative work ships small improvements continuously, learning from each cycle. The opposite is 'big bang' — designing everything upfront and shipping once. Iteration is foundational to agile.
words.professionalContext: An iterative rollout — 1%, then 10%, then 100% — catches issues at low blast radius.
words.exampleSentence: The team's iterative approach turned a six-month project into a series of weekly wins.
words.etymology: From Latin iterare, 'to repeat' — same root as 'reiterate'.
words.connector: Like editing a draft — each pass makes it sharper.

words.shortDefinition: use something to maximum advantage
words.simpleDefinition: Get a big result from a small investment.
words.longDefinition: Leverage is amplification — small input, outsized output. In engineering, leverage shows up as automation, tooling, and platforms that multiply individual effort.
words.professionalContext: Building internal tooling has high leverage — one engineer's work makes hundreds more productive.
words.exampleSentence: She leveraged the existing job framework to ship the new feature in days.
words.etymology: From the physics of levers — small force, large displacement.
words.connector: Like a lever — short pull, heavy lift.

words.shortDefinition: make less severe or serious
words.simpleDefinition: Reduce a problem's impact when you can't fully eliminate it.
words.longDefinition: Mitigation accepts that risk exists and reduces its likelihood or blast radius. A mitigation isn't a fix — it's a damping mechanism for a known issue you can't fully prevent.
words.professionalContext: Rate limiting mitigates the risk of API abuse without preventing it entirely.
words.exampleSentence: Adding a circuit breaker mitigated the cascading failure risk.
words.etymology: From Latin mitigare, 'to soften, soothe' — same root as 'mild'.
words.connector: Like a shock absorber — bumps still happen, but you feel less of them.

words.shortDefinition: considering the whole rather than parts
words.simpleDefinition: Looking at the big picture, not just one piece.
words.longDefinition: Holistic thinking treats systems as integrated wholes where parts affect each other. The opposite is reductionist — optimizing one component at the expense of overall system behavior.
words.professionalContext: A holistic view of latency considers network, backend, database, and client — not just one layer.
words.exampleSentence: Her holistic redesign improved both performance and developer experience together.
words.etymology: From Greek holos, 'whole, entire' — same root as 'whole'.
words.connector: Like ecology — every species affects every other.

words.shortDefinition: able to move and adapt quickly
words.simpleDefinition: Quick to change direction when reality changes.
words.longDefinition: Agile (lowercase) means responsive and flexible. Agile (capitalized) is a software methodology valuing working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change over rigid plans.
words.professionalContext: Agile teams adapt scope mid-sprint when learnings invalidate assumptions.
words.exampleSentence: Her agile response to the outage — rolling back, then root-causing — earned the team's trust.
words.etymology: From Latin agilis, 'nimble' — capable of being moved easily.
words.connector: Like a sailboat that tacks with the wind, not against it.

words.shortDefinition: a temporary supporting structure
words.simpleDefinition: A skeleton structure to build on or work from.
words.longDefinition: In code, scaffolding is generated boilerplate (file structure, basic classes) that gives you something to start from. As a verb, to scaffold is to create that supporting structure intentionally.
words.professionalContext: We scaffolded the new service with the standard internal template — auth, logging, and metrics out of the box.
words.exampleSentence: Rails scaffolds CRUD endpoints with one command.
words.etymology: From Old French eschafaud, 'a temporary platform' — used in construction.
words.connector: Like the framework around a building under construction.

words.shortDefinition: change direction strategically
words.simpleDefinition: Switch to a different approach when the original isn't working.
words.longDefinition: Pivot describes a deliberate strategic change — usually keeping some elements (team, tech, learnings) while changing others (product, market, approach). It implies pivoting on something stable.
words.professionalContext: After three months of low adoption, the team pivoted from a consumer app to a B2B tool using the same backend.
words.exampleSentence: She pivoted from individual contributor to engineering manager after the org restructure.
words.etymology: From French pivot, 'a fixed point on which something turns' — like a basketball pivot foot.
words.connector: Like turning on one heel — anchor stays, direction changes.

words.shortDefinition: a starting point for comparison
words.simpleDefinition: Where you start so you can measure progress later.
words.longDefinition: A baseline is a reference measurement — without it, 'we improved performance' is meaningless. Establishing baselines is a discipline of mature engineering teams.
words.professionalContext: Always capture a baseline before starting a perf project — otherwise you can't claim a win.
words.exampleSentence: Their baseline showed p99 latency at 800ms; six weeks later it was 200ms.
words.etymology: From the literal 'base line' in surveying — the reference line measurements are taken from.
words.connector: Like a starting line in a race.

words.shortDefinition: a significant point in development
words.simpleDefinition: A marker that says 'we made it this far'.
words.longDefinition: Milestones break long projects into measurable chunks with clear definitions of done. They serve both as progress markers and motivation — finishing a milestone provides closure that 'we worked on this for six months' lacks.
words.professionalContext: Setting milestones at 25%, 50%, and 75% gives the team three completion celebrations instead of one distant launch.
words.exampleSentence: The first milestone — feature parity with the old system — took longer than expected but caught critical bugs.
words.etymology: From the literal milestones along Roman roads marking distance.
words.connector: Like checkpoints in a video game — confirms you're making progress.

words.shortDefinition: designate or treat as more important than others
words.simpleDefinition: Decide what gets done first — and what gets dropped.
words.longDefinition: True prioritization isn't ranking everything as P0; it's saying no to things. The hardest part is choosing not to do work that's clearly valuable when something more valuable competes.
words.professionalContext: Prioritization is leadership's hardest job — saying yes is easy, saying 'not this quarter' is what creates focus.
words.exampleSentence: She prioritized the security fix over the performance work, even knowing perf was visible to executives.
words.etymology: From Latin prior, 'former, earlier' — what comes first.
words.connector: Like packing one suitcase for a long trip — what stays out matters as much as what goes in.

words.shortDefinition: based on observation or experience rather than theory
words.simpleDefinition: Backed by evidence and data, not just opinion.
words.longDefinition: Empirical claims rest on what's been measured or observed. The empirical engineer asks 'what did the experiment show?' before forming an opinion. Often invoked to dismiss arguments that lack evidence.
words.professionalContext: Empirical proof beats architectural elegance — load test results trump 'it should be fast'.
words.exampleSentence: Her empirical analysis showed the cache hit rate was 60%, not the assumed 95%.
words.etymology: From Greek empeirikos, 'experienced' — knowledge from experience rather than theory.
words.connector: Like a lab experiment — data over dogma.

words.shortDefinition: a practical rule of thumb that usually works
words.simpleDefinition: A shortcut rule that's usually right but not guaranteed.
words.longDefinition: Heuristics trade optimality for speed. They're approximations that work well enough most of the time. Engineering is full of heuristics — 'cache invalidation is hard', 'premature optimization is the root of all evil'.
words.professionalContext: A useful heuristic: if a query takes more than 100ms, profile it before assuming the database is fine.
words.exampleSentence: His heuristic for code review — does it match the surrounding style? — caught most issues fast.
words.etymology: From Greek heuriskein, 'to find' — same root as 'eureka!'
words.connector: Like a recipe approximation — not exact measurements, but it works.

words.shortDefinition: separate two things that were connected
words.simpleDefinition: Break the dependency between two things so they can change independently.
words.longDefinition: Decoupling reduces the cost of change. When systems are tightly coupled, modifying one forces modifying the other; when decoupled, they evolve independently. Often achieved via interfaces, queues, or events.
words.professionalContext: Decoupling the analytics service from the main API let us redeploy each on its own schedule.
words.exampleSentence: We decoupled the frontend from the backend by introducing a stable API contract.
words.etymology: Latin de- (apart) + Old French copler 'to bind' — to unbind.
words.connector: Like detaching a trailer — each can go its own way.

words.shortDefinition: made up of independent, interchangeable parts
words.simpleDefinition: Built from separate pieces you can swap or rearrange.
words.longDefinition: Modular systems isolate concerns into well-defined units with clear boundaries. Modularity supports parallel development, easier testing, and selective replacement — but adds upfront design cost.
words.professionalContext: A modular codebase lets new engineers contribute to one area without understanding the whole system.
words.exampleSentence: The modular auth library plugged into three different services with minor configuration.
words.etymology: From Latin modulus, 'a small measure' — pieces of a defined size.
words.connector: Like LEGO blocks — combine as you need.

words.shortDefinition: able to recover quickly from setbacks
words.simpleDefinition: Bounces back when things go wrong instead of staying broken.
words.longDefinition: Resilience isn't about preventing failure — it's about recovering gracefully when failure happens. Resilient systems degrade rather than crash; resilient teams learn from incidents rather than assigning blame.
words.professionalContext: Resilient services use retries with backoff, circuit breakers, and fallbacks for downstream failures.
words.exampleSentence: The resilient design kept core checkout working even when recommendations were down.
words.etymology: From Latin resilire, 'to leap back' — same root as 'resile'.
words.connector: Like a tree that bends in a storm but doesn't break.

words.shortDefinition: able to grow without breaking
words.simpleDefinition: Keeps working when 10× the users show up.
words.longDefinition: Scalability is the ability to handle increased load by adding resources — not by rewriting. A scalable system grows linearly with capacity; a non-scalable one hits a wall and needs redesign.
words.professionalContext: A scalable approach to background jobs uses a queue + workers, not cron + database polling.
words.exampleSentence: The new architecture is scalable up to 100k requests per second without code changes.
words.etymology: From Latin scala, 'ladder' — climbing higher rungs.
words.connector: Like a highway with extra lanes — adding lanes adds capacity.

words.shortDefinition: capable of working successfully
words.simpleDefinition: Realistic and likely to actually succeed.
words.longDefinition: A viable option is feasible given real constraints — time, budget, capability. Not optimal, not perfect, but workable. The 'V' in MVP (Minimum Viable Product) carries this weight.
words.professionalContext: The proposal was technically interesting but not viable — we don't have the team to maintain it.
words.exampleSentence: Of three architectures considered, only one was viable within the timeline.
words.etymology: From French viable, 'able to live' — same root as 'vital'.
words.connector: Like a seed planted in fertile soil — it can actually grow.

words.shortDefinition: extremely thorough and careful
words.simpleDefinition: So thorough nothing slips through the cracks.
words.longDefinition: Rigor is precision plus discipline — checking edge cases, verifying assumptions, citing evidence. Rigorous engineering takes longer up front and saves time later by preventing surprises.
words.professionalContext: A rigorous postmortem doesn't stop at 'the deploy broke prod' — it traces back to why the test missed it.
words.exampleSentence: Her rigorous review caught the off-by-one no one else noticed.
words.etymology: From Latin rigor, 'stiffness, severity' — disciplined to the point of unyielding.
words.connector: Like a chess master analyzing every move — nothing skipped.

words.shortDefinition: strong and able to handle adversity
words.simpleDefinition: Holds up under stress, weird inputs, and edge cases.
words.longDefinition: Robust systems handle the unexpected — malformed input, network glitches, unusual user behavior — without crashing. Robustness goes beyond 'works on the happy path' to 'works in the wild'.
words.professionalContext: Robust input validation rejects unexpected types instead of trusting and crashing later.
words.exampleSentence: The robust deserializer handled malformed JSON without taking down the service.
words.etymology: From Latin robustus, 'oaken, strong' — like an oak tree.
words.connector: Like a Land Rover — built to handle terrain that breaks lesser vehicles.

words.shortDefinition: a typical pattern or model of thinking
words.simpleDefinition: A way of thinking that shapes how you approach a whole class of problems.
words.longDefinition: Paradigms are mental frameworks that determine what questions you ask and what answers count. Object-oriented, functional, and reactive are all programming paradigms — each shapes how you decompose problems.
words.professionalContext: Switching from imperative to functional is a paradigm shift, not a syntax change.
words.exampleSentence: The microservices paradigm trades development complexity for operational complexity.
words.etymology: From Greek paradeigma, 'pattern, example' — the model that others follow.
words.connector: Like switching languages — same thoughts, but a different way of forming them.

words.shortDefinition: combined effect greater than the sum of parts
words.simpleDefinition: Two things working together produce more than they would separately.
words.longDefinition: Real synergy means 1 + 1 = 3. The word is overused in corporate-speak, but the concept matters: combining two systems or teams can multiply output if their strengths complement each other.
words.professionalContext: There was real synergy between the design system team and the platform team — both made each other faster.
words.exampleSentence: The synergy between the new caching layer and the query optimizer cut p99 latency by 60%.
words.etymology: From Greek synergia, 'working together' — syn (with) + ergon (work).
words.connector: Like a band — drums alone, vocals alone, but together they make a song.

words.shortDefinition: not dependent on or restricted to a specific platform or technology
words.simpleDefinition: Doesn't care which underlying system you use — works with any.
words.longDefinition: An agnostic design works regardless of platform, vendor, or implementation. Cloud-agnostic code runs on AWS, Azure, or GCP. Database-agnostic code works with Postgres or MySQL. Often achieved through abstraction layers.
words.professionalContext: Vendor-agnostic infrastructure trades best-in-class features for the freedom to switch providers.
words.exampleSentence: We kept the storage layer cloud-agnostic so we wouldn't get locked into S3.
words.etymology: From Greek agnōstos, 'unknown, unknowing' — by extension, indifferent to specifics.
words.connector: Like a power adapter that works in any country.

words.shortDefinition: increasing in size or effect over time
words.simpleDefinition: Small things that grow huge because each gain builds on the last.
words.longDefinition: Compounding is the engine of long-term value — small consistent improvements that build on themselves. In engineering, compounding shows up in good test coverage, well-named code, and team practices that improve onboarding for everyone who comes after.
words.professionalContext: Investing in tooling has compounding returns — every engineer added benefits, not just today's team.
words.exampleSentence: His compounding investment in documentation made the codebase legendary for ramp speed.
words.etymology: From Latin componere, 'to put together' — gains placed on top of gains.
words.connector: Like interest in a savings account — yesterday's gains earn today's gains.
